name: The Pulse Quickens
description: Pilot Kira has been recording timing intervals since before she understood what she was measuring. The intervals have collapsed — what was a rhythm is now nearly continuous. She needs to tell Maren: the fourteen months may be fewer.
accept_text: She has the logbook open to the most recent pages and the recent pages look different from the older ones. The older ones are columns of numbers — interval lengths, measured to the second, carefully spaced. The recent pages are almost solid ink. 'The intervals have stopped being intervals.' She says it without looking up. 'Eight months ago I was recording gaps of forty to sixty seconds between pulse peaks. Six months ago: twenty seconds. Three months ago: eight seconds.' She closes the logbook. 'Yesterday I stopped counting seconds between peaks and started counting peaks per minute. The number I'm getting is not a rhythm. It's a frequency.' She looks at you. 'Something in the model has changed. The acceleration Maren and Yael calculated assumed the current rate of healing. But the current rate of healing is not the rate I'm measuring. It's faster.' She picks up a fresh notebook — the kind she takes on long flights, dense paper, small lines. 'The fourteen months they calculated — I think the actual number is smaller. I don't know by how much. That's a question for Maren.' She hands you the recent logbook pages, copied onto a single sheet. 'Take these to her. Tell her: the intervals collapsed faster than the model predicted. Tell her I need a revised estimate.' She pauses. 'And tell her: I'm not worried. I want to be clear about that. I've been sitting with this pulse for eighteen months and whatever it is, I'm glad it exists. I just want to know when.'
complete_text: Maren looks at the numbers and does not speak for thirty seconds. Then she gets up and goes to the instruments. She runs the same calculation she ran the night the Watcher arrived, with the new pulse frequency substituted for the old one. The result appears on the calculation board. She looks at it. She runs it again. The result is the same. 'Nine months.' She says it quietly. 'Not fourteen. Nine. The acceleration isn't linear — it's geometric. Each increment of coherence enables faster coherence.' She looks at the Watcher's home position in the sky, already settled. 'I built the model on a linear assumption because I didn't have enough data points to identify the curve.' She pauses. 'I have them now.' She writes the revised number at the top of a clean page and underlines it twice. 'Nine months from the Watcher's arrival.' She does the arithmetic. 'Which means approximately six months from now.' She looks at you. 'This changes things. We have been working as though there is time to explain everything slowly and carefully to everyone who needs to understand it. Six months is not slow.' She sits down. 'I need to get word to Arlen. To Stone. To the Keeper — the twelve voices need to know the timeline they've been working toward is sooner than we said.' She pauses. 'And I need to tell Yael. She calculated on my data. Her model is wrong by the same margin mine was.' She picks up the pen. 'Six months. The Grid reaches full coherence in six months.' She writes it down as if writing it makes it real in a way that calculating it didn't quite. 'We should have started some of these conversations earlier.'
